title: Design Critique — Structured Feedback for Better Work
content: A design critique produces clear, actionable feedback when properly structured. Before: designer shares context (goals, constraints, audience, stage), defines what feedback is needed, sets rules (constructive, specific, actionable). During: 1) Present (5 min) — designer walks through work and goals. 2) Clarify (5 min) — questions to understand, not judge. 3) Feedback rounds — structured by category or priority. 4) Discuss — open conversation on key tensions. 5) Capture — document decisions and action items. Feedback format: 'I notice...' (observation), 'I wonder...' (question), 'What if...' (suggestion), 'I think... because...' (opinion with rationale). After: designer summarises takeaways, action items get owners and deadlines. Types: desk crit (informal 1-on-1), team crit (scheduled, structured), cross-team crit (fresh eyes), stakeholder review (decision-focused). Separate exploration critiques from refinement critiques. Critique the work, not the person. Tie feedback to goals and user needs, not personal preference.

title: Design QA Checklist — Verifying Implementation Accuracy
content: Systematic QA ensures implementations match design specifications. Categories: Visual accuracy (colours match tokens, typography matches styles, spacing/sizing correct, border radius/shadows/opacity, icons correct, image aspect ratios). Layout (grid alignment, responsive behaviour at each breakpoint, proper reflow, no overflow/clipping, min/max widths). Interaction (all states render — default/hover/focus/active/disabled, transitions match specs, touch targets 44px minimum, keyboard navigation order, visible focus indicators). Content (real content fits layout, truncation works, empty states display, error messages correct, loading states appear). Accessibility (screen reader announces correctly, colour contrast WCAG AA, focus management works, ARIA labels/roles correct, reduced motion respected). Cross-platform (required browsers, required devices, OS text size settings, screen densities). Process: developer self-review → designer visual QA → file bugs with screenshots comparing design vs implementation → prioritise → verify fixes. QA against the spec, not memory.

title: Design Review Process — Quality Gates Without Bottlenecks
content: Review gates maintain quality at the right checkpoints. Gate 1 — Concept Review: problem defined, user needs supported by research, multiple concepts explored, strategic alignment, stakeholder input. Gate 2 — Design Review: visual design meets brand standards, interaction patterns consistent, responsive behaviour defined, content strategy applied, design system components used. Gate 3 — Pre-Handoff: all states designed (empty, loading, error, success), edge cases addressed, accessibility met, specs complete, developer walkthrough done. Gate 4 — Implementation QA: design matches spec, interactions work, responsive verified, accessibility tested, cross-browser checked. Criteria: does it solve the user problem? Consistent with design system? Accessible (WCAG AA)? All states covered? Feasible to implement? Approval workflow: self-review → peer review → lead sign-off → stakeholder approval → developer acceptance. Scale the process to project size and risk — not every project needs every gate. Time-box reviews. Document decisions.

title: Design Sprints — Challenge to Tested Prototype in Five Days
content: The design sprint (Jake Knapp, Google Ventures) takes teams from challenge to tested prototype in a focused week. Day 1 Understand: define challenge and sprint questions, expert interviews, map user journey, choose focus area. Day 2 Diverge: lightning demos, individual sketching (Crazy 8s, solution sketches), silent critique and heat map voting, choose direction. Day 3 Decide: review solutions, storyboard prototype flow, assign roles, plan testing. Day 4 Prototype: build realistic façade prototype, divide and conquer, stitch together and rehearse, confirm logistics. Day 5 Test: 5 user interviews with prototype, observe and note, debrief after each, synthesise patterns, decide next steps. Variations: mini sprint (2–3 days), remote sprint (digital tools), discovery sprint (days 1–2 only). Requirements: decision maker in the room, 5–7 cross-functional team members, dedicated space, users recruited for day 5. No devices during working sessions. Plan the follow-up before the sprint ends.

title: Developer Handoff — Complete Implementation Specifications
content: Handoff documents give developers everything needed for accurate implementation. Contents: Visual specs (spacing/sizing as token references, colours as token names not hex, typography style/size/weight/line-height, border radius/shadows/opacity, responsive breakpoints). Interaction specs (state definitions — default/hover/focus/active/disabled, transitions with duration/easing/properties, gesture behaviours, keyboard interactions and tab order). Content specs (character limits and truncation, dynamic content rules with min/max, localisation considerations including text expansion and RTL, empty/loading/error state content). Asset delivery (SVG icons named per convention, images with resolution/format/responsive variants, fonts, custom illustrations). Edge cases (min/max content scenarios, responsive behaviour per breakpoint, browser/device considerations, accessibility — ARIA, keyboard, screen reader). Implementation notes (component reuse, data structure assumptions, API dependencies, performance considerations). Use design tokens, not raw values. Annotate behaviour, not just appearance. Walk through handoff with the developer.

title: Design Team Workflows — Rituals, Tools, and Collaboration
content: Effective workflows balance structure with flexibility. Task management: how work is tracked (boards, tickets, sprints), status definitions (backlog → in progress → review → done), priority levels, capacity planning. Collaboration rituals: standup (daily/async — working on, blockers), design critique (weekly — structured feedback), design review (per milestone — quality gate), retrospective (per sprint — process improvement), show and tell (bi-weekly — share with broader team). Communication norms: sync vs async, response time expectations, how to request feedback, how to share decisions, documentation requirements. Tooling stack: design (Figma), prototyping, project management (Jira/Linear), communication (Slack/Teams), documentation (Notion/Confluence). Design-development collaboration: when designers join ceremonies, handoff timing, QA process, bug reporting, shared component library management. Workflow stages: discovery → exploration → refinement → handoff → QA → iteration. Review and adapt regularly. Optimise for actual needs, not theory.

title: Design Version Control — Files, Libraries, and Tokens
content: Version control strategy for design assets ensures collaboration and consistency. What to version: design files, component libraries, design tokens, icon sets, documentation. Design files: named versions at milestones (v1-exploration, v2-refinement, v3-final), branch-based (main for approved, feature branches for WIP), page-based (history within file using pages). Component libraries: semantic versioning (major.minor.patch) — major for breaking changes (renamed components, removed props), minor for new components (backward compatible), patch for bug fixes. Design tokens: version alongside component library, changelog documenting additions/changes/removals, migration guides for breaking changes. Branching: main = production-ready approved designs, feature branches = WIP, review before merging, archive old versions rather than delete. Changelog practices: document what changed and why, link to design decisions, note breaking changes prominently, include migration instructions. Communicate changes to consumers (developers, other designers).
